InnerSeed Garden · UX Development Manifesto · Week 3

Closing the Loop —
15–18 June 2026

Week 3 opens by closing out the remaining Preset mode questions carried over from Week 2, then turns to Builder mode as the week's headline focus. Along the way, fresh evidence sharpens two existing findings, several new findings are logged, and a proposed full-screen "cinematic mode" is set out for the Preset player.

WeekWeek 3
Date15 June 2026
AuthorRuaraidh
Appinnerseed.garden
Carries forwardD-01 to D-38
StatusComplete
Session context. The final items left open from Week 2 — the Preset session completion flow and Filter mode — were revisited this week. The completion flow remains untested for reasons set out below; in the course of that testing, several new pieces of evidence emerged, including confirmation that the Week 2 language and metadata concerns (D-03, D-16) are systemic rather than isolated. A proposed "cinematic mode" for the Preset player is also set out, ahead of Builder mode becoming the headline focus of the remainder of the week.
01

Week 3 agenda

Agreed running order for the week, in sequence:

  • Close out Preset mode — confirm the session completion flow and test Filter mode within the browsing flow
  • Builder mode — full UX audit, the headline focus of the week
  • Mobile testing, all fronts — stress-testing the Week 2 findings on a touch screen
  • Analytics and human testing — groundwork for Week 4 delivery
  • Comms and findings integration plan — scope to be confirmed before drafting

Keep it simple, stupid — still the guiding principle

Still open from Week 2

No notes yet from the Friday 13 June focus call. Jeroen's response to D-03 (Critical, Dutch language issue) and the D-21 threefold consistency proposal remains the most useful single input still to be brought into the record, and may reshape the priority order above.

02

Closing out Preset mode — session completion flow

The first item carried over from Week 2 was confirming what happens when a Preset session reaches its end. This could not be completed this week.

"Couldn't do this right now as there is no movement on the scrubber (this has been deliberate) because we don't want people starting and stopping a session and just losing interest. It's better to give them variety, and urge them to complete each session rather than halt their experience (this is the logic for now!)"
Finding D-22 — Session completion flow remains untested

The progress scrubber cannot be dragged, by design — confirmed as a deliberate choice intended to discourage starting and stopping sessions and to encourage full completion. This is a reasonable rationale on its own terms, but it also means there is currently no practical way to reach or preview the end of a session without playing one through in full, and no short-duration Preset exists to make that quick to do.

Recommendation: agree a way to review the completion state without committing a full 20+ minute play-through each time — for example a short test/preview Preset, or a developer-only fast-forward option. Until this is resolved, the question first raised in Week 2 (what a user sees and where they land when a session ends) remains open, and connects directly to D-13 (no way back from the player to the dashboard).

03

Preset mode — default state and session selection

With the completion flow set aside for now, attention turned to Preset mode's default (no session selected) state, and to how a session is actually chosen.

"Let's look at the preset mode in desktop without anything being selected... This is fair enough and does make sense for now (to be revisited later) and when clicking on the box 'choose preset' this is what's given... That does make sense (to be revisited in next sessions for certainty)"

Fig. 1 — Preset mode with no session selected — "Choose a preset to begin," with a "Choose preset" prompt inside a dashed placeholder

Noted, acceptable for now

The empty state communicates clearly enough that a session needs to be chosen, and was accepted as reasonable without further findings at this stage. Flagged to revisit once Filter mode and the full session library have been tested in more depth.

Clicking the "Choose preset" prompt opens the Load Session modal already documented in Week 2 (D-19, D-20). The same modal reappeared here, but with an unexpected difference worth recording in detail.

Fig. 2 — Load Session modal, Presets tab — session descriptions displayed in Dutch

Fig. 3 — The same modal, same three presets, displayed in English

Finding D-23 — D-03 confirmed at the content level, not just the page level

Figures 2 and 3 show the identical Load Session modal, listing the same three presets — Breathe Deep Loop, Monsoon Vault, Ethereal Voyager — with their descriptions rendering in Dutch in one instance and in English in another. This confirms that the language issue first logged as D-03 (Critical) is not limited to a browser translation prompt firing on page load. The underlying session content itself is stored or served in more than one language, inconsistently.

Recommendation: this raises the priority of D-03 further. The developer check should now extend beyond the HTML lang attribute and page metadata, to how session description content is stored and served — specifically why the same three presets return different language content across instances.

Supporting detail

Outside of the description text, the metadata line in both languages correctly reads "22 min · Connect · ♂ Jeroen" for Breathe Deep Loop — without the "CONTACT ·" prefix seen on the player view. This detail becomes significant in the next section.

04

Metadata anomaly — confirmed and pinpointed

Selecting Breathe Deep Loop and opening its player view reproduced the same metadata anomaly first logged in Week 2 against Ethereal Voyager.

Fig. 4 — Breathe Deep Loop player banner — "22 min · CONTACT · Connect · ♂ Jeroen"

Finding D-24 — D-16 confirmed across multiple presets, and the fault is now located

The "CONTACT · Connect" anomaly first logged against Ethereal Voyager (D-16) now reproduces identically against a second preset, Breathe Deep Loop — confirming this is systemic rather than a one-off rendering issue tied to a single session.

More usefully, comparing this against Figures 2 and 3 pinpoints exactly where the fault sits. The Load Session modal's list view renders the same field correctly, as "Connect" alone, for the same preset. The fault is therefore isolated to the player banner's rendering of this field, not to the underlying data — which narrows the developer's search considerably.

Recommendation: compare the player banner template against the Load Session list-item template for this field specifically; the list-item version is the correct reference implementation.

05

Banner image — affordance and meaning

While moving between presets, a further piece of behaviour came to light: clicking the large artwork banner above the player reopens the Load Session modal, allowing a different preset to be chosen.

"I noticed that you need to click on the banner to swap the preset (that's cool but not obvious and only figured because I am click happy as a UX tester)... what the banner actually is remains unclear — it could be populated with something less graphic, or something more graphic in line with the garden or inner seed"
Finding D-25 — Banner click-to-swap is functional but undiscoverable

The banner artwork doubles as a control for changing the active preset, which is a sensible re-use of screen space. However, nothing about its current appearance signals that it is interactive — it reads as decorative artwork, not as a button. This was only discovered through exploratory testing, and a typical user has no reason to expect a large image to be clickable.

Separately, the visual content of the banner itself (an abstract gradient, currently unrelated to InnerSeed's garden identity) does not reinforce either the app's branding or its function. There is an opportunity to address both issues together: a clearer visual cue that the banner is interactive, and artwork more deliberately aligned with the InnerSeed Garden identity.

Recommendation: add a visible affordance (e.g. a small "change session" label or icon on the banner) distinct from the artwork beneath it, and consider artwork more thematically tied to the garden identity rather than generic abstract gradients.

06

Sidebar during playback — towards a "cinematic mode"

Switching between presets surfaced a new addition to the sidebar — an inline mode-switcher panel — and prompted a wider question about how much of the interface should remain visible once a session is actually playing.

Fig. 5 — Sidebar showing the inline Preset / Builder / Creator switcher panel, with a "Recent" section beneath it

First reaction, noted as-is

Initial reaction to this addition was neutral — functional, with no strong view either way at first glance. After clicking through the three options, switching between modes from this panel was found to work smoothly.

"I ended up here when playing ethereal voyager, and went between two different presets without too much difficulty... in short the side bar menu sits open, as in the full slider is open, and doesn't shrink when the preset is playing — personally I think the screen should be as 'cleared' as possible for something like breathwork... the visuals should speak for the overall energy you would like to create for a client in person, which is minimal distraction, calming and sound on the eyes and ears, so maybe the side bar could disappear altogether and the screen could really transform into a 'full screen cinematic mode' which also adds oomph for the user who is going on a journey, right? They should be transported to the beginning of their journey and sent on their way."

Fig. 6 — Sidebar remains fully expanded throughout playback — visible alongside the player at all times, regardless of session progress

Finding D-26 — Sidebar and transport chrome remain visible throughout playback

The sidebar stays fully expanded for the entire duration of a session, with no transition to a calmer or more focused state once playback begins. This compounds two existing findings: D-06 (tonal mismatch — the product reads as "tech/gaming" rather than calming) and D-15 (the player doesn't visually read as a breathwork session). For a wellness product whose stated purpose is present-moment awareness, an interface that keeps navigation chrome on screen throughout the experience works against that purpose.

Design grounding for the proposal below

A small number of established principles support reducing the interface during active playback. Hick's Law holds that decision time increases with the number of choices presented, so removing all but the single most relevant action during a session shortens and calms that decision-making moment. The concept of calm technology (Case, 2015) argues that good technology should sit at the edge of a person's attention rather than demanding it directly, which supports replacing buttons and labels with a single ambient visual wherever possible. Sweller's cognitive load theory similarly supports minimising on-screen elements that are not directly relevant to the task at hand, reducing the mental effort required to simply be present in a session rather than operate an interface.

Based on this, a proposed "cinematic mode" was sketched: when a session starts, the sidebar and transport chrome recede, and the screen transitions into a single full-screen visual — concentric circles pulsing in a slow, breath-paced rhythm, a quiet countdown, and the visual itself acting as the only control, with a tap anywhere on it pausing or resuming playback.

Fig. 7 — Before — current player view, sidebar and full transport bar visible throughout

Fig. 8 — Proposed — full-screen cinematic mode. No sidebar, no progress bar, no separate buttons. A single countdown, and the visual itself as the only control

Finding D-26, proposed direction — "cinematic mode"

On entering playback, the interface recedes entirely: sidebar, transport icons, metadata line and progress scrubber are all removed from view. In their place, a single full-screen visual — breathing rings, pulsing in time with the guided pace, with a quiet countdown beneath — fills the screen. The visual itself becomes the only control: tapping it pauses or resumes the session, removing the need for separate play, pause and stop icons altogether. A single, unobtrusive way to exit (returning to the dashboard) remains available throughout.

This is offered as a direction for discussion rather than a finished specification. Open questions to settle before this is taken further: whether the exit point should be a small icon as shown, or a different gesture entirely; whether the countdown should be more prominent or recede further into the background; and whether this treatment should extend to Builder and Creator sessions once they are actively playing, or remain specific to Preset playback.

Aesthetic treatment of players — broader note

Separately from the structural threefold consistency work already proposed for navigation (D-21), a related point was raised regarding the visual appeal of the player and preset selection screens themselves across Builder and Creator modes, not only Preset. This is logged here as a standing consideration to return to once Builder mode has been audited in full later this week, so that any visual treatment proposed is grounded in how all three modes actually look and feel, not Preset mode alone.

07

Builder mode — first glance

Switching briefly into Builder mode via the panel in Figure 5 gave an early look ahead of the full Builder mode audit planned for later this week.

Fig. 9 — Builder mode, first glance — "Build your session," with Ambient Music, Guided Breaths and Guided Voices surfaced as the three content pillars

Preview note — not yet a full finding

This was a brief look rather than a structured audit. The three content pillars (Ambient Music, Guided Breaths, Guided Voices) are surfaced directly as toggles, which is a useful early signal for the fuller Builder mode audit planned next. No findings are logged against Builder mode yet — that work begins properly once Preset mode is fully closed out.

08

"Recent" — discoverability and behaviour

The "Recent" section visible in the sidebar (Figure 5) was explored briefly to understand what it tracks and how useful it could become.

"I just saw the 'recents' part added to the menu bar on the left, which is cool and I want to spend a minute exploring what that could look like, and how it could be super useful"

Fig. 10 — "Recent" still showing "No sessions played yet" after Ethereal Voyager had already been played more than once in the same sitting

Finding D-27 — "Recent" does not appear to register plays

Despite Ethereal Voyager being played more than once during this session, "Recent" continued to display "No sessions played yet." It is not yet clear whether this requires a full completion to register (which would connect directly to D-22, since completion currently cannot be tested), or whether it is not yet tracking plays at all.

Recommendation: confirm with the developer what is intended to trigger a "Recent" entry, and once known, retest once a full session completion can be achieved.

Finding D-28 — minor labelling inconsistency

The same section is labelled "Recent" in most views (Figures 5, 6, 10) but "Recents" in the default Preset-mode empty state (Figure 1). A small inconsistency, but worth standardising to one label given the broader consistency theme already raised in D-21.

Finding D-29 — sidebar items appear inconsistently across otherwise similar screens

Comparing screens captured at different points this week, a "Green House" navigation item appears in the sidebar in some Preset-mode views and is absent in others, with no clear rule governing when it shows. This may be intentional (context-dependent navigation) or an inconsistency in how the sidebar renders. Flagged for developer clarification, and connects to the wider structural concern already raised in D-21 — the sidebar is one of three navigation systems already shown not to behave consistently.

Potential, once working as intended

A working "Recent" list could meaningfully support a returning user who wants to pick up where they left off, without needing to browse or filter — directly relevant to the discovery-first principle established in Week 2, and to D-08 (no single-click path to "experience something").

09

Running findings log — D-22 to D-38

RefFindingTypePriorityRecommendation
D-22Session completion flow could not be reached this week — scrubber intentionally locked, no short Preset for a quick previewUX / Testing constraintMediumResolved via natural play-through — see D-30
D-23Same Load Session modal, same presets, render in Dutch in one instance and English in anotherTechnicalCriticalEscalates D-03 — investigate content-level language handling, not only page metadata
D-24"CONTACT · Connect" metadata anomaly reproduced on a second preset; correct in the modal list view, faulty only in the player bannerTechnicalHighConfirms and pinpoints D-16 — compare banner template against the correctly-rendering modal list template
D-25Banner artwork is clickable (swaps preset) but gives no visual indication of this; artwork unrelated to garden identityUX / AffordanceMediumAdd a visible "change session" affordance; consider on-brand artwork
D-26Sidebar and transport chrome remain visible throughout playback; no immersive state during a sessionUX / Visual DesignHighProposed "cinematic mode" — full-screen visual, single tap-to-pause control, see Section 06
D-27"Recent" still reads "No sessions played yet" after multiple plays of the same sessionTechnicalMediumConfirm what triggers a "Recent" entry; retest now that completion is possible
D-28Sidebar section labelled "Recent" in most views, "Recents" in the default empty Preset stateUX / ConsistencyLowStandardise to a single label
D-29"Green House" sidebar item present in some Preset-mode views, absent in others, with no clear ruleUX / ConsistencyMediumDeveloper to confirm whether this is intentional; connects to D-21 and D-31
D-30Major — Session completion is under-acknowledged; only on-screen change is the timer resetting and a single "Go To Green House" prompt appearing, with no acknowledgment that the session has endedUX / CompletionHighReconsider what (if anything) should mark completion before any CTA is presented
D-31Major — "Green House" and "Garden" read as overlapping, undifferentiated concepts; the term itself carries connotations that work against the Garden identityStrategy / Branding & IACriticalNow partly resolved by D-36 — see below; naming question remains for Thursday's discussion
D-33Major — Immediately after completion, no on-screen control responded except "Go To Green House" — a single forced path with no alternativeUX / CompletionCriticalEscalates D-30 — reconsider locking the interface to one action straight after an intensive session
D-34Settings and save icons disappear from the transport bar after completion and remain missing even when returning via RecentTechnicalHighConfirm with developer whether intentional or unintended state loss
D-35Load Session modal content is unstable — preset count, descriptions and the third tab's label ("Shared With me" vs "Shared By me") all changed across viewingsTechnicalCriticalEscalates D-23 — investigate as one underlying data/caching issue rather than isolated symptoms
D-36Major — Green House finally opened: a stats and community hub ("Your Rhythm," a 12-month activity heatmap, Progress/Coaches/Events tabs), not a duplicate of the Garden dashboardDiscovery / StrategyHighResolves the structural half of D-31; naming question remains, sharpened by the literal greenhouse backdrop artwork
D-37Sidebar nav item labelled "Session Builder" in earlier views, "Sessions" in later onesUX / ConsistencyMediumFourth instance of the same labelling-instability pattern; connects to D-21, D-28, D-29
D-38Recent list item rendering is inconsistent — correct with clock icon in its first appearance, concatenated as "21 minJeroen" with no separator in later viewsTechnicalMediumLikely a template/formatting issue specific to certain render contexts
10

Sources & references

UX research grounding

Nielsen, J. (1994) 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design, Nielsen Norman Group.

Nielsen Norman Group (2018) Scrolling and Attention.

Tognazzini, B. (1998) The "Illusion of Completeness."

Case, A. (2015) Calm Technology: Principles and Patterns for Non-Intrusive Design.

Sweller, J. (1988) Cognitive Load During Problem Solving: Effects on Learning.

Hick, W. E. (1952) On the Rate of Gain of Information — basis for Hick's Law.

11

Week 3 plan of attack — remaining steps

With Preset mode now closed out as far as this week allows, the remaining sequence for Week 3 is as follows.

Step 1 — Builder mode: full UX audit (headline focus)

Build a session from scratch, live, applying the same structured lens used for Preset mode in Week 2 — clarity, orientation, friction — continuing the D-xx findings log. Particular attention to whether Builder mode shows the same "three disconnected navigation systems" pattern (D-21), and to the aesthetic point raised in Section 06 regarding how the Builder and Creator players look, not only how they function.

Step 2 — Mobile testing, all fronts

Re-run both the Preset and Builder journeys on a mobile device, as a stress test of the findings already logged — particularly D-04 (hover-only navigation) and D-21 (three disconnected navigation systems) — rather than as a separate, standalone audit.

Step 3 — Analytics and human testing: groundwork

Both planned now for delivery in Week 4. The human testing brief will be written once Builder mode and mobile testing are complete, so it reflects the final state of the findings log; Ruaraidh to arrange participants and logistics. The analytics framework remains a "what to measure" list tied to the findings already logged (D-18 favourites, D-23/24 metadata and language, D-27 Recent), rather than a technical build specification.

Step 4 — Comms and findings integration plan

Earlier drafts of this plan referred to integrating findings into "the back garden" — this has since been corrected; the intended reference throughout was always the Greenhouse. With Green House now opened and its content understood (D-36), this item can move from an unscoped placeholder to an actual conversation: how findings and comms should be reflected within the Greenhouse's existing structure (Your Rhythm, Progress, Coaches, Events). Raised directly in the Thursday 18 June focus call.

12

Session completion — observed live

Ethereal Voyager was allowed to play through to the end this session, finally answering the question carried over since Week 2 as D-22.

Fig. 11 — The only change on screen once the session ends: the timer resets to 00:00, and a "Go To Green House" prompt appears beneath the transport bar

"I'm not sure I understand why I should go to the green house if I'm honest — it seems a bit anticlimactic just now, and I want to know where I am with myself. I have just done a really intensive breathwork session, so coming back to a screen isn't that easy or what I want to do, so I think I might leave it or just close it before even doing anything."
Finding D-30 — Session completion is under-acknowledged

On completion, the timer resets to its starting position and a single "Go To Green House" prompt appears — the only change anywhere on screen. There is no acknowledgment that a session has actually ended, no summary, and no sense of arrival. Coming directly out of an intensive breathwork session, this was experienced as anticlimactic, and the more likely reaction was to close the app rather than engage with a further decision.

Recommendation: reconsider what, if anything, should mark the moment of completion before any call to action is presented. This connects to D-13 (no way back from the player to the dashboard) and to the discovery-first principle established in Week 2 — the end of a session is as important a moment to design for as the beginning.

13

Naming conflict — "Green House" and "Garden"

The completion prompt directed attention to a question that had been circling since D-29: what the Green House actually is, and how it relates to the wider Garden identity.

"What is the green house, and isn't that the garden? There seems to be a crossover or merging of concepts here that needs addressed... the green house needs replaced with garden — we grow things in a garden — a greenhouse is suffocating for a human — we don't feel natural when we hear greenhouse — it's too familiar with greenhouse gases."
Finding D-31 — "Green House" and "Garden" read as two competing, overlapping concepts

It is not currently clear whether the Green House is a destination within the Garden, a separate space entirely, or another name for the same thing. Beyond the structural ambiguity, the term itself works against the brand: "greenhouse" suggests an enclosed, artificial growing environment, which sits at odds with the open, natural quality the Garden identity is built around.

This connects directly to D-29 (the same nav item appearing inconsistently across screens) and to D-30 above, since the post-session prompt asks the user to go somewhere whose purpose and relationship to the rest of the app has not yet been established.

Recommendation: agree with Jeroen what the Green House is intended to represent, and consider whether the Garden identity alone should hold this role rather than introducing a second, competing concept. A written summary of this point has been prepared separately for Jeroen ahead of the Thursday 18 June discussion.

Outstanding before this can be resolved

The Green House destination itself has not yet been opened or tested. Any naming or structural recommendation should be confirmed against its actual content before being finalised.

14

Immediately after completion — a locked interface

A closer look at the moment of completion turned up something more serious than D-30 alone suggested.

"YOU ARE FORCED TO LEAVE THE PRESET AND GO TO GREEN HOUSE — AFTERWARDS (WHEN IT FINISHED) NONE OF THE BUTTONS WORKED OTHER THAN [Go To Green House]."
Finding D-33 — Only one control remains responsive after completion

In the moments immediately after Ethereal Voyager finished, no on-screen control responded except the "Go To Green House" prompt itself — not the transport icons, not the artwork, nothing else on the player. This goes beyond D-30's point about the moment being under-acknowledged: the interface is not simply quiet at this point, it is actively restricting the user to a single path forward, with no alternative offered.

Recommendation: escalates D-30. Coming directly out of an intensive personal experience, being funnelled into one unavoidable choice is a meaningful trust concern for a wellness product specifically, regardless of intent. Worth discussing whether this is a deliberate design decision and, if so, whether it should be reconsidered.

Fig. 12 — The transport bar after completion — only the folder, play and stop icons remain; settings and save are both missing

Finding D-34 — Settings and save icons disappear after completion

Beyond the Green House prompt itself, the transport bar changes shape: the settings (cog) and save icons are no longer present, leaving only folder, play and stop. This was still the case when returning to the player later via the Recent list, suggesting it is a persistent state change rather than a momentary one.

Recommendation: confirm with the developer whether this is intentional (for example, settings and save being considered irrelevant once a preset has been completed) or an unintended loss of state.

15

Load Session modal — content found to be unstable

Opening the session picker again later in the same testing session, via the folder icon, surfaced further inconsistency beyond the Dutch/English issue already logged as D-23.

Fig. 13 — The same modal, later in the same session — only one preset listed, a different description ("The Garden's First Breath"), and the third tab now reading "Shared By me" rather than "Shared With me"

Finding D-35 — Load Session content is unreliable across viewings

In Week 2 and earlier this week, the Presets tab consistently listed three sessions (Breathe Deep Loop, Monsoon Vault, Ethereal Voyager). Later in this session, the same tab listed only Ethereal Voyager, with a different description entirely — "The Garden's First Breath" rather than the earlier "Your first breath journey made with AI music" (or its Dutch equivalent). The third tab's label also changed, from "Shared With me" to "Shared By me" — not a cosmetic difference, since the two phrases describe different relationships to shared content.

Recommendation: escalates D-23. The pattern now extends well beyond language alone — preset availability, descriptions and tab labelling all appear unstable across sessions. This points toward a single underlying data or caching issue rather than several unrelated symptoms, and is worth investigating as one ticket rather than three.

16

Green House — finally seen

Following "Go To Green House" through for the first time answers the question raised in D-31, at least in part.

Fig. 14 — Green House — "Your Rhythm" (sessions completed, sessions today, day streak), a twelve-month "Breathwork Moments" activity record, and three sub-sections: Progress, Coaches, Events

Finding D-36 — Green House is a distinct stats and community space, not a duplicate dashboard

Green House turns out to be a genuine, separate feature — a personal progress hub showing session counts, a day streak, and a long-range activity record similar in spirit to a habit-tracking calendar, alongside two further sections (Coaches, Events) suggesting a community or guidance dimension beyond solo practice. This resolves the structural half of D-31: Green House is not simply a confusingly-named duplicate of the Garden home screen, and there is a legitimate, distinct piece of functionality here.

The naming concern in D-31 stands regardless, and arguably sharpens: the backdrop artwork behind this screen is a literal glass greenhouse structure, which leans further into the connotation the original objection raised, rather than away from it. The question for Thursday's discussion shifts from "what is this?" to "does this deserve to be a separate, named space — and if so, is 'Green House' still the right name, or would something aligned with the Garden identity (for example, framing it as part of the Garden rather than a separate destination) serve the brand better?"

Recommendation: bring this screen into the Thursday discussion directly, now that its content is known. This sits at the centre of the comms and findings integration question raised in the Week 3 plan (see Notes, below).

17

Further consistency findings

Fig. 15 — Sidebar reading "Sessions" rather than "Session Builder," and a Recent entry rendering as "21 minJeroen" with no separator

Finding D-37 — A fourth inconsistent label

The sidebar item used to navigate into Builder mode reads "Session Builder" in earlier screenshots and simply "Sessions" in later ones (including within Green House). This is the fourth instance of the same labelling-instability pattern already logged for this app — alongside D-21 (structural inconsistency across modes), D-28 ("Recent" vs "Recents"), and D-29 ("Green House" appearing inconsistently in the sidebar).

Finding D-38 — Recent list item rendering is inconsistent

The first time a Recent entry appeared, it rendered correctly — a clock icon followed by "21 min." In later views (inside Green House, and after returning to the player via Recent), the same entry instead read "21 minJeroen" — duration and host name run together with no separator and no icon. Likely a formatting issue specific to certain render contexts rather than a single consistent bug.

D-32, refined

Earlier this week, Recent appeared to lose its history after navigating into Session Builder and back. In this session, Recent persisted correctly through a different route — Preset to Green House, clicking the Recent entry, and back to Preset. This suggests the loss may be specific to navigating into Sessions/Builder mode rather than all navigation. Worth retesting deliberately during the Builder mode audit: does Recent survive a Garden–Green House round trip but not a Garden–Sessions round trip?

18

Focus call, 18 June 2026 (Part 1) — Green House, explained

Jeroen's own account of Green House's purpose, given directly in today's call, answers the question raised in D-31 far more fully than testing alone could.

"Alignment: feedback notes are being processed, and when they align with the other contributor's and J's thoughts, they are used and implemented. So this is live and being used — great from a uni perspective, and should be highlighted when it is used for the e-portfolio.

J wants to really remove the noise and focus mainly on the breath and the three-part story.

My articulating past, present, future — there is nothing wrong with you.

Root, Rise, Return are principles, and are also the philosophy of life: learn/grow, rise/live, grow kids/return/come down/die. So this three-part cycle is life, and what is missing is how it plays into connection, and when you engage to learn or do something. So the Greenhouse is somewhere that you can go beyond the practice, beyond the breath, and connect on a deeper level with people that you can trust, because like any internal work, it's difficult, so we need hugs, warmth, connection — just like a men's group, or somewhere that friends meet, or a small garden where people meet to have a talk therapy session. They are safe spaces, and that's what this is. Kasht means vault, or commitment vault, in Dutch, whereby the subscription fee goes into that vault to make the user come back. Greenhouse can have that concept as well, in terms of a digital space for your brain that you can spend money on, grow certain aspects of your life, and actually do the work that you would usually do in a typical space.

Might be a cool idea.

Madeira — botanical gardens, Funchal gardens — indoor spaces."

D-31, resolved

The naming and conceptual question raised on 17 June is now answered directly by Jeroen, rather than inferred from testing alone. Green House is a deliberate space, distinct from the practice of breathwork itself: somewhere to connect more deeply with trusted others, in the spirit of a men's group, a circle of friends, or a small garden where people gather for talk therapy. Root, Rise and Return are not only structural principles but a philosophy of life — learning and growing, living, and eventually settling and passing on — and Green House is where the "connection" dimension of that cycle is meant to live, alongside the practice itself.

The Dutch concept of "kasht" (vault, or commitment vault) was also raised as a possible model: a subscription fee held as a kind of ongoing personal commitment, encouraging return engagement, which could extend naturally to Green House as a space for sustained personal investment and growth.

This resolves the conceptual half of D-31 fully — Green House is not redundant with Garden, and has a thought-through philosophical basis. Whether "Green House" remains the right name for it, given the connotation concern raised on 17 June, is a smaller, separate question that can now be considered on its own terms, with the concept itself no longer in doubt.

19

Vision and future direction

The latter part of the call moved beyond the current UX audit into where the app could go next.

"Moving forward, we are going to do a little more research around some of the things that were talked about today. We want to look at digital spaces and the sort of direction that AI and meta are taking us. I think it's very wise to let the imagination run its due course in advance and really look to see where this app could go. Obviously, this is a breathwork app, so we want to start looking at the competition — what other people are doing, just in case there's something too similar, and what people are not doing — and try to draw some inspiration from the vast world out there. Then think about what the Garden could be, what the Greenhouse could be. Can this be a place where people go to develop themselves, to work on themselves, just by putting a pair of glasses on? Where is the subscription model going, in terms of the world? Where are people going to be going to do their breathwork in ten years, or five years? Can this be digitalised? Can this be more than a breathwork app? Because I think this is where things get really interesting, and I think that's where a lot of the future development can go, in terms of what this report might reflect, and how the time is spent on the Manifesto after the app has been given its full UX report and is on the road to being what J set out to do. But this is where I come in — there has to be more. What will sell, what can be developed. This document needs a lot of thought from you, and guidance for me, to think deeper and follow the three key philosophies — Root, Rise and Return — for a greater development of the app."
Agreed direction for future research

Once the current UX audit and the immediate app fixes are further along, research is to begin on the wider competitive and technological landscape: how comparable breathwork and wellness products are positioned, where genuine gaps in the market sit, and what direction digital and AI-driven experiences (including spatial computing — Jeroen's reference to "putting a pair of glasses on") are heading. This sits alongside longer-range questions: how subscription models for this kind of product may evolve, what breathwork practice might look like in five to ten years, and whether Garden and Green House could grow into something broader than a breathwork app.

This is logged here as the agreed starting point for that strand of work, to be developed through the Root, Rise and Return philosophy as a throughline, and to inform how time is allocated on the Manifesto once the current UX phase is further along.

20

Session Builder settings icon — a note on expert design

Alongside the live testing captured in this week's sessions, a separate written observation was produced on the settings cog icon within the Session Builder interface. It is included here in full as a standalone analytical note, grounded in established usability theory, and sits alongside the findings log rather than within it — it is an interpretation of a pattern, not a new isolated finding.

Observation — Session Builder settings icon

The Session Builder interface presents a clean, minimal, and visually sophisticated design language. However, from a first-time user perspective, the settings cog located alongside "Session Builder" introduces a small but meaningful usability challenge. The issue is not that the cog icon is unfamiliar — the gear metaphor is one of the more widely recognised symbols for settings. The challenge is that the meaning of this particular instance is unclear within the context of the application. A new user may reasonably ask: "What am I changing if I click this?" The interface communicates that something can be configured, but not what type of configuration is available.

UX principle — recognition over recall

A foundational principle in usability design holds that interfaces should prioritise recognition over recall: users should not have to remember, infer, or guess what an element means. The interface should provide enough contextual information for users to understand available actions immediately (Nielsen, 1994). The cog communicates that it is interactive and relates to configuration. It does not communicate whether it controls session-building options, audio preferences, advanced controls, or application-level settings. The user must form a mental model before taking action.

Mental models and signifiers

Norman's work on human-centred design distinguishes between affordance — what an element suggests can be done with it — and signifier, the visual cue that communicates what it does and how it should be used (Norman, 2013). The cog's affordance is clear: "you can interact with this." Its signifier is incomplete: "what interaction will happen?" For a first-time user, this gap creates friction before the session-building process has even begun.

The expert user gap

A recurring challenge in software design is the gap between expert users — who understand interface conventions and established workflows — and new users, who rely on obvious contextual cues and guidance. A minimalist interface can become highly efficient for experienced users while simultaneously increasing uncertainty for newcomers. The InnerSeed Garden Session Builder, as currently designed, reflects a product built by a technically sophisticated developer with a clear internal model of how the application works. That internal model does not yet fully translate to a user encountering the interface for the first time. This is not a criticism of the design intent; it is a common and well-documented stage in the development of professional-grade software, and it is precisely the kind of gap a UX consultancy process is designed to surface and address.

Cognitive load

Cognitive load theory suggests that working memory has limited capacity (Sweller, 1988). Every moment spent interpreting an interface adds a small but real cognitive cost: what does this icon mean, is it important, should it be clicked first, will it change the session? For a meditation and wellbeing-focused application, reducing unnecessary uncertainty is particularly important — the desired emotional state is calm, confidence, and ease. Unexplained elements work against that.

Recommendations

Add a short contextual label alongside or beneath the cog — "Session Settings," "Session Options," or "Builder Options" — to remove the interpretive burden without altering the visual aesthetic. Where screen real estate makes a permanent label undesirable, progressive disclosure via a tooltip or hover state achieves the same result with no added visual noise. The guiding principle: minimal design should remove unnecessary elements, not necessary understanding.

Additional references for this observation: McDougall, S.J.P., Curry, M.B. and de Bruijn, O. (2000) 'The effects of visual information on users' mental models: an evaluation of icon design.' Nielsen Norman Group research on icon usability, recognition versus interpretation, and first-time user experience.

—

Closing Week 3 — what moves into Week 4

Week 3 is now complete. The items below summarise what was resolved this week, what carries forward, and what shifts into Week 4 and beyond. The sequencing below is proposed and fluid — it will be refined in the light of the 18 June focus call with Jeroen and any subsequent direction from ISD. Builder mode work began at the close of this week and continues as the headline focus of Week 4, carrying the same structured lens and D-xx findings log established across Weeks 2 and 3. All work referenced here took place in line with the direction set during the 18 June focus call; visual representations produced during the development process were created with the support of visual prototyping tools used for the purpose of application development.

1. Preset mode — complete

The session completion flow was observed in full and logged (D-22, D-30, D-33, D-34). Green House was opened and its purpose understood (D-36). The cinematic mode proposal was developed and documented (D-26). Filter mode was deliberately deferred to mobile testing, where it falls naturally within the Preset browsing flow — a considered sequencing decision, not an oversight.

2. Builder mode — carries into Week 4 (headline focus)

A first look at the Builder interface was completed at the close of Week 3, producing initial findings (D-39 to D-43 — see Week 4 Manifesto). The full structured audit — building a session from scratch, applying the same clarity/orientation/friction lens used across Weeks 2 and 3, with visual representations produced for development reference where findings connect back to D-21 — continues as the headline focus of Week 4. The Week 3 focus call directly informed the framing for this work, particularly Jeroen's direction to reduce noise and foreground the breath and the three-part Root/Rise/Return story.

3. Mobile testing — planned for Week 4/5

Mobile testing will re-run both the Preset and Builder journeys on a touch device, as a deliberate stress test of existing findings — particularly D-04 (hover-only navigation, which does not exist on mobile) and D-21 (three disconnected navigation systems, likely to become more pronounced on a small screen). Filter mode, deferred from Preset mode above, will also be tested within the mobile Preset browsing flow at this stage. Timing is fluid and will be confirmed in the weekly focus call.

4. Analytics and human testing — planned for Week 4/5

Both follow the completion of Builder mode and mobile testing. The human testing brief will be written once the full findings picture is available — the approach is to test with a clear hypothesis rather than in open-ended discovery, which requires the audit to be substantially complete first. The analytics framework remains a "what to measure" list linked directly to findings (D-18/19/20/21, D-27, D-32, D-38), not a technical build specification. Logistics to be arranged by Ruaraidh; timing to be confirmed in the focus call.

5. Comms and findings integration plan — scoped

Earlier drafts of this plan referenced "the back garden" — corrected throughout; the reference was always the Greenhouse. With Green House now opened, understood, and contextualised directly by Jeroen in the 18 June focus call as a deliberate community and connection space aligned with the Root/Rise/Return philosophy, this item has moved from an unscoped placeholder to a live conversation. How findings and communications integrate into the Greenhouse's existing structure (Your Rhythm, Progress, Coaches, Events) is now part of the active development discussion.

6. Still open — carried into Week 4

D-03 (Critical, Dutch language and content instability, now escalated through D-23 and D-35) remains the single highest-priority technical item. D-21 (threefold consistency) remains the single highest-priority structural recommendation. Both were raised in the 18 June focus call. Builder mode audit and mobile testing are the next substantive steps. The Friday 13 June focus call notes have not been formally logged — if available, they should be added to the appendix session log before the Week 4 Manifesto is finalised.

InnerSeed Garden · UX Development Manifesto · Week 3 · Ruaraidh · 15–18 June 2026 · Complete innerseed.garden
